Product.title: Product
Product.description: Represents a single pastry item available for sale in the Haya Tunis Pastries catalog.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Product entity.

description.description: A short, appetizing description of the pastry.

imageUrl.description: The URL of the image displaying the pastry.

price.description: The current selling price of the pastry.

Order.title: Order
Order.description: Represents a customer's validated purchase of pastries from Haya Tunis Pastries.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Order entity.

orderDate.description: The date and time when the order was placed.

totalAmount.description: The total monetary amount of the order, including all items.

status.description: The current status of the order (e.g., 'pending', 'confirmed', 'shipped', 'delivered', 'cancelled').

customerName.description: The name of the customer who placed the order.

customerEmail.description: The email address of the customer.

customerPhone.description: The phone number of the customer.

shippingAddress.description: The shipping address for the order (e.g., '123 Main St, Anytown, 12345').

orderItemIds.description: References to OrderItem entities associated with this order. (Relationship: Order 1:N OrderItem)

OrderItem.title: OrderItem
OrderItem.description: Represents a specific product and its quantity within a customer's order.

id.description: Unique identifier for the OrderItem entity.

orderId.description: Reference to the Order this item belongs to. (Relationship: Order 1:N OrderItem)

productId.description: Reference to the Product being ordered. (Relationship: Product 1:N OrderItem)

quantity.description: The quantity of the product ordered.

unitPrice.description: The price of the product at the time it was added to the order. This can differ from the current product price.

definition.description: Collection storing all available pastry products. Publicly readable by all users. Write access is restricted to administrators only.

Collection storing all customer orders. Each document includes a denormalized 'customerId' field for authorization independence, linking it to the user who placed it. Customers can read/write their own orders. Administrators can read all orders to manage them.

Subcollection storing the individual items for a specific order. Each document includes a denormalized 'customerId' field for authorization independence, inherited from its parent order, allowing atomic access checks.

params.name: orderId
params.description: The unique identifier of the parent Order.

definition.description: Collection for assigning administrative roles. The existence of a document at this path for a given UID signifies that the user is an administrator (DBAC).

params.name: uid
params.description: The Firebase User ID of the administrator.

firestore.reasoning:
The proposed Firestore structure prioritizes Authorization Independence, Structural Segregation, and Access Modeling as mandated. It ensures a scalable, secure, and debuggable database schema for Haya Tunis Pastries, particularly addressing the new admin page requirement.

**Authorization Independence via Denormalization:**
Crucially, to eliminate `get()` calls in security rules and support atomic operations, authorization context is denormalized. Each `Order` document will include a `customerId` field (matching `request.auth.uid` for authenticated users, or a unique anonymous ID for guests). Similarly, each `OrderItem` document within an order's subcollection will also include this `customerId`, copied directly from its parent `Order`. This allows security rules for both `Orders` and `OrderItems` to directly check `resource.data.customerId == request.auth.uid` for customer-specific access, avoiding expensive and dependency-creating `get()` operations on parent documents or user profiles.

**Structural Segregation for Homogeneous Security Posture:**
*   `/products`: This collection holds all product information. It has a homogeneous security posture: public read access for all users (for browsing the catalog) and restricted write access for administrators only. This clear separation simplifies rules significantly.
*   `/orders`: This top-level collection holds all customer orders. Its security posture allows customers to create new orders and read their *own* orders (via the denormalized `customerId` field). Administrators have global read access to this collection, facilitating the 'validated orders' view for the admin page. The `status` field (`'validated'`, `'pending'`, etc.) allows for easy filtering without impacting security.
*   `/orders/{orderId}/orderItems`: This subcollection stores the individual items associated with a specific order. Its security posture is inherently tied to its parent `Order` but is made authorization-independent through the denormalized `customerId` field in each `OrderItem` document. This allows for fine-grained, atomic access checks even at the item level if needed.
*   `/roles_admin/{uid}`: This collection is dedicated to managing administrative roles. Its security posture is based on existence: if a document with a user's `uid` exists here, that user is an administrator. This is a clean, DBAC-compliant method for role management, separate from user profiles.

**QAPs (Rules are not Filters) Support:**
*   **`/products`:** Supports QAPs directly. Any user can `list` all documents in `/products` because the data is public. Security rules will simply allow `read` access for `list` operations.
*   **`/orders`:** 
    *   For customers, `list` operations will explicitly require a `where('customerId', '==', request.auth.uid)` clause in their queries. The security rules will allow `list` only if this specific filter is present and matches the authenticated user's ID, thus remaining QAP-compliant by preventing arbitrary unfiltered `list` operations for non-admins.
    *   For administrators (identified by `roles_admin/{uid}`), `list` operations on `/orders` are fully allowed, enabling the admin page to retrieve all orders (e.g., `db.collection('orders').where('status', '==', 'validated').get()`). This is QAP-compliant as admins have unrestricted list access to this data.
*   **`/orders/{orderId}/orderItems`:** Similar to `/orders`, customers' `list` access will be implicitly filtered by their `customerId` on the parent order path, and explicitly secured by the denormalized `customerId` on the `OrderItem` itself. Admins will have full `list` access to `orderItems` under any order.
